item.title: Trusting agents with enterprise data
item.description: An execution model for autonomous agents that survives enterprise security review.

item.title: Retrieval you can audit
item.description: Document intelligence where every answer carries its provenance.

item.title: Fifteen services, one install
item.description: Delivering a fifteen-service data platform into customer Kubernetes environments in one step.

item.title: A lockless hash table in a database core
item.description: Inside SAP HANA, where a single data structure tripled parallel query throughput.

item.title: Robots that made burgers
item.description: Directing the software for autonomous burger assembly at Momentum Machines.

item.title: Managing engineers who never sleep
item.description: A year of running AI coding agents turned into an accidental management apparatus, written one rule at a time.

item.title: What it takes to let agents touch enterprise data
item.description: Trust in agentic systems is an architecture property, not a model property.

item.title: Your gold set is lying to you
item.description: Reference answers are artifacts with bugs, and they are the only software in the stack that nobody code-reviews.

item.title: An answer you cannot audit is worth nothing
item.description: Provenance is a data-model property, not a UI feature, and it cannot be retrofitted.

item.title: Authorization for answers
item.description: We have learned to secure what agents do. The harder question is what they are allowed to know.

item.title: Evaluating systems that answer in sentences
item.description: Evaluation infrastructure is the difference between a demo and a product.

item.title: LLM spend is an attribution problem
item.description: A month of anonymous model spend taught me that cost governance for AI is identity infrastructure, and the discipline that fixes it already has a name.

item.title: Data infrastructure and AI infrastructure are different disciplines
item.description: They optimize for different things, fail in different ways, and the hard problems live at the seam.

item.title: Why your knowledge graph isn't helping your RAG
item.description: We nearly published the fashionable verdict that GraphRAG is hype. The audit found something better, the structural reasons the published architectures break on enterprise data.

item.title: The physical world does not retry idempotently
item.description: What burger-making robots taught me about agents acting on production data.

item.title: The model designs, the code enforces
item.description: In an LLM ingestion pipeline, the model gets the judgment that requires reading the document and nothing else. The surprise is how badly the prompt wants to violate that in both directions.

item.title: Database lessons for the agent era
item.description: Agent platforms are rediscovering, one incident at a time, what database engines settled decades ago.

item.title: Tables are images
item.description: Meaning in a table lives in its geometry, and the architecture that survives a real corpus is a per-table router, not an ideology.

item.title: What regulation does to architecture
item.description: Years of healthcare engineering taught me that compliance, taken seriously, is a design input that produces better systems.

item.title: When documents become databases
item.description: Schema discovery is a corpus-statistics problem before it is a modeling problem.

item.title: Free like a puppy, times fifteen
item.description: A product assembled from open source is not the sum of its components. It is the resolution of their disagreements.

item.title: Forcing the race
item.description: Race conditions are not hard to test. They are untested, because teams accept probabilistic reproduction.

item.title: About
item.description:
I work at the convergence of data infrastructure and AI, as a founding engineer on an agentic data fabric platform for enterprises with complex operational data estates. My scope runs from API contracts and identity models through agent execution, retrieval, evaluation, and Kubernetes delivery, plus the hiring and the standards that keep all of it coherent.

The arc that got me here is unusual on purpose. I started inside the SAP HANA database core, building lockless data structures and chasing nanoseconds with SIMD and RDMA. Then a robotics startup, directing the software that assembled burgers autonomously. Then a big-data platform at SparklineData that Oracle acquired, and Spark-as-a-Service for Fortune 500 workloads at Oracle itself. Then engineering leadership in healthcare: telehealth at eVisit, health-equity fintech at Soda Health. In between, I founded Lakeshore Labs, an AI agents consultancy serving enterprises across Southeast Asia and the Gulf.

item.title: Resume
